_meta.note: Static lookup used by /predict to enrich the model's class output into the rich AnalysisResult shape the frontend expects. Each entry corresponds to one class name in class_indices.json. severity_baseline is in 0-100 and is scaled by model confidence at inference time.

Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It produces olive-green to brown velvety lesions on leaves and fruit and is the most common apple disease in cool, wet climates.
symptoms: Olive-green to dark brown spots on upper leaf surface
Lesions becoming corky and cracked on fruit
Premature leaf drop
Distorted leaf growth
treatment: Apply fungicide such as captan or myclobutanil at green tip and repeat every 7-10 days during wet weather
Remove and destroy fallen leaves to reduce overwintering inoculum
Prune to improve air circulation
prevention: Rake and dispose of fallen leaves each autumn

progression.timeline: Days 1-7: small olive lesions on young leaves

environmentalFactors.watering: Water at the base; avoid wetting foliage

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is a fungal disease caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa. It causes leaf spots ('frogeye'), branch cankers, and fruit rot.
symptoms: Purple-bordered circular leaf spots that turn brown ('frogeye')
Sunken brown cankers on branches
Black, mummified fruit
Concentric rings on rotting fruit
treatment: Prune out dead wood and cankers, removing 6 inches below visible damage
Apply fungicide (captan or thiophanate-methyl) at petal fall and repeat
Destroy mummified fruits and infected debris
prevention: Maintain tree vigour with balanced fertilization
Avoid bark wounds during pruning and harvest

progression.timeline: Days 1-7: small purple spots on leaves
Weeks 2-4: cankers form, fruit rot begins

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: A fungal disease (Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae) requiring both apple and cedar/juniper hosts. Causes bright yellow-orange spots on apple leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Bright yellow-orange leaf spots with red border
Tube-like structures on underside of leaves
Fruit deformation and lesions
Premature defoliation
treatment: Apply fungicide (myclobutanil or mancozeb) starting at pink bud stage
Remove nearby cedar/juniper galls if possible
Continue sprays through petal fall
prevention: Maintain >2 km from cedar/juniper hosts where feasible
Prune cedars to remove galls in late winter

progression.timeline: Days 1-10: small yellow spots after spring rain
Weeks 2-4: spots enlarge, orange tubes form

Apple___healthy.description: The leaf appears healthy. No disease symptoms detected.
treatment: No treatment required
prevention: Prune annually for good air circulation

treatment: No treatment required
prevention: Mulch with pine needles or sawdust

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: A fungal disease (Podosphaera clandestina) producing white powdery growth on cherry leaves and shoots.
symptoms: White powdery patches on leaves and stems
Distorted, curled leaves
Stunted shoot growth
Russeting on fruit
treatment: Apply sulfur or potassium bicarbonate fungicide at first signs
Prune affected shoots to improve airflow
Repeat applications every 7-14 days
prevention: Plant in full sun with good air movement

progression.timeline: Weeks 2-3: spreads across leaves and shoots
Week 4+: distortion, leaf drop, fruit russeting

environmentalFactors.humidity: Moderate (favours dry leaves with humid air)

treatment: No treatment required

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is a fungal disease (Cercospora zeae-maydis) producing rectangular tan lesions on corn leaves, capable of significant yield loss.
symptoms: Small pinpoint lesions that elongate into rectangles
Tan to gray rectangular lesions parallel to leaf veins
Lesions merging into large blighted areas
Lower leaves affected first
treatment: Apply foliar fungicide (azoxystrobin, propiconazole) at tasseling if disease pressure is high
Time second application 10-14 days later if conditions warrant
prevention: Rotate corn with non-host crops (soybean) for 1-2 years

progression.timeline: Days 1-14: pinpoint lesions on lower leaves

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. Cinnamon-brown pustules appear on both leaf surfaces.
symptoms: Small cinnamon-brown pustules on both upper and lower leaf surfaces
Pustules turn dark brown to black as they mature
Yellow chlorotic flecks around pustules
In severe cases, leaf death
treatment: Apply foliar fungicide (azoxystrobin, mancozeb) if rust appears before tasseling
Generally a fungicide is only economical on susceptible hybrids under heavy pressure
prevention: Avoid late planting in rust-prone areas

progression.timeline: Week 4+: black telia form, leaves die

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern corn leaf blight (Exserohilum turcicum) causes long, cigar-shaped grayish-green lesions on corn leaves.
symptoms: Long elliptical (cigar-shaped) lesions, 2.5-15 cm
Lesions tan to gray-green, with parallel sides
Lesions may coalesce, blighting leaves
Lower leaves affected first
treatment: Foliar fungicide (azoxystrobin, propiconazole) at early tasseling
May need a second application 14 days later in heavy pressure
prevention: Crop rotation away from corn for 1-2 years

treatment: No treatment required

Grape___Black_rot.description: Black rot of grape (Guignardia bidwellii) is a serious fungal disease that mummifies fruit and causes leaf spots.
symptoms: Tan to brown circular leaf spots with dark borders
Tiny black dots (pycnidia) within spots
Fruit rot turning black and mummified
Cane lesions
treatment: Apply fungicide (mancozeb, myclobutanil) starting at 2-3 inch shoot growth
Continue every 10-14 days through veraison
Remove mummified fruit promptly
prevention: Remove all mummified berries from canes and ground

progression.timeline: Days 1-14: leaf spots on new growth

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca / Black Measles is a complex fungal disease (multiple Phaeoacremonium and Phaeomoniella species) of mature grapevines causing wood decay and leaf/berry symptoms.
symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern between leaf veins (yellow on white grapes, red on dark)
Small dark spots on berries ('measles')
Sudden vine collapse in hot weather (apoplexy)
Wood necrosis when canes are split
treatment: No reliable cure once vine is infected; remove and destroy severely affected vines
Trunk renewal by training a new shoot from below the diseased trunk can sometimes save a vine
prevention: Make pruning cuts in dry weather and protect with sealant

progression.timeline: Year 4+: vine collapse during hot spells

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Isariopsis leaf spot (Pseudocercospora vitis) causes dark angular spots and premature defoliation on grape leaves.
symptoms: Angular dark brown to black leaf spots
Spots bounded by leaf veins
Yellow halos around lesions
Premature defoliation in heavy infections
treatment: Apply mancozeb or copper-based fungicide at first symptoms
Repeat every 10-14 days during wet weather
prevention: Train vines for good air movement

treatment: No treatment required

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Citrus greening (HLB), caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter spp. and spread by Asian citrus psyllid, is the most destructive disease of citrus worldwide. There is currently no cure.
symptoms: Asymmetrical yellow blotchy mottle on leaves
Misshapen, lopsided green fruit (does not colour properly)
Bitter, salty fruit
Twig dieback and tree decline
treatment: No cure available
Aggressively control psyllid vectors with insecticides
Improve nutrition (foliar micronutrient sprays) to prolong tree productivity
Remove and destroy heavily infected trees to reduce inoculum
prevention: Monitor for and control Asian citrus psyllid
Report suspect trees to local agricultural authorities

progression.timeline: Months 1-6: blotchy mottle on individual branches
Years 1-2: spread throughout canopy, fruit drop

environmentalFactors.watering: Maintain good irrigation to support stressed trees

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: A bacterial disease (Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni) affecting peach leaves, twigs and fruit, especially in warm humid climates.
symptoms: Small, angular, water-soaked leaf spots that turn purple
Spots drop out leaving 'shot-hole' appearance
Sunken pits and cracks on fruit
Premature defoliation
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides at petal fall and shuck split
Continue applications every 7-10 days during wet weather
prevention: Fertilize moderately - excess nitrogen worsens disease

treatment: No treatment required
prevention: Annual pruning for shape and airflow
Thin fruit to reduce branch breakage

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot (Xanthomonas spp.) is a serious disease of peppers in warm humid weather, causing leaf spots, defoliation, and fruit lesions.
symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked leaf spots that become brown
Yellow halos around lesions
Lesions on fruit becoming raised and scabby
Premature leaf drop reducing fruit quality
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides at first sign
Combine copper with mancozeb for better effect
Avoid handling plants when wet
prevention: Rotate with non-host crops for 2 years
Stake plants to keep foliage off the soil

environmentalFactors.sunlight: Full sun (but defoliation causes sunscald on fruit)

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: The bell pepper leaf appears healthy.
treatment: No treatment required
prevention: Mulch to retain moisture and prevent soil splash

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight (Alternaria solani) is a common foliar disease of potato that produces concentric-ring leaf lesions. It usually starts on older leaves.
symptoms: Brown circular lesions with concentric rings (target spots)
Yellowing around lesions
Older leaves affected first
Defoliation up the stem in heavy infections
treatment: Apply fungicide (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) at first symptoms
Repeat every 7-10 days in wet weather
Remove severely affected leaves
prevention: Rotate crops 2-3 years away from solanaceous plants
Mulch to prevent soil splash onto leaves
Maintain plant vigor with balanced fertility

progression.timeline: Days 1-7: small dark spots on lower leaves

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is a devastating water mold disease responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It can destroy a crop in days under cool, wet conditions.
symptoms: Pale-green water-soaked lesions on leaf margins
Lesions rapidly turning brown to black
White fuzzy sporulation on undersides in humid weather
Foul-smelling tuber rot
treatment: Apply protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) preventively
Switch to systemic fungicide (e.g., metalaxyl) once disease appears
Destroy infected plants immediately to prevent spread
prevention: Hill soil over tubers to protect from spores
Destroy volunteer potatoes and cull piles

progression.timeline: Days 1-3: pale lesions on leaf tips
Week 2+: total foliage collapse, tuber rot

treatment: No treatment required
No treatment required
prevention: Train canes on a trellis for airflow

treatment: No treatment required
prevention: Rotate with corn or small grains
Scout fields weekly through reproductive stages

environmentalFactors.watering: Rainfed in most climates; supplement during pod fill

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew (Podosphaera xanthii / Erysiphe cichoracearum) is the most common cucurbit foliar disease, producing white powdery growth on leaves and stems.
symptoms: White powdery patches on upper leaf surface
Yellowing leaves that brown and die
Stunted plant growth
Reduced fruit yield and quality
treatment: Apply potassium bicarbonate, sulfur, or neem oil at first signs
Repeat every 7-10 days
Remove most-affected leaves to slow spread
prevention: Control summer weeds that harbour the fungus

progression.timeline: Days 1-7: small white spots on older leaves

environmentalFactors.humidity: Moderate (favours dry leaves with humid air)

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch (Diplocarpon earlianum) is a fungal disease of strawberry causing reddish-purple leaf spots that resemble scorching.
symptoms: Numerous small purple to reddish spots on leaves
Leaves taking on a scorched, burned appearance
Spots may merge into large blighted areas
Reduced runner production and yield
treatment: Apply captan or myclobutanil fungicide
Begin applications at early bloom and continue at 7-10 day intervals
Remove old infected leaves after harvest
No treatment required
prevention: Mulch with straw to keep berries clean

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot (Xanthomonas spp.) of tomato causes dark leaf spots, defoliation, and fruit lesions, particularly in warm wet weather.
symptoms: Small dark water-soaked leaf spots, often with yellow halo
Lesions on stems and fruit
Fruit lesions becoming raised and scabby
Defoliation reducing fruit quality
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides at first sign
Combine copper with mancozeb for better suppression
Avoid working in fields when wet
prevention: Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 2-3 years
Stake plants and mulch to reduce splash

environmentalFactors.sunlight: Full sun (sunscald risk on defoliated plants)

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight (Alternaria solani / A. tomatophila) is a very common tomato disease causing concentric-ring lesions on lower leaves first.
symptoms: Brown circular lesions with concentric rings (target spots)
Yellowing around lesions
Lower leaves affected first
Stem cankers and fruit lesions near the calyx
treatment: Apply fungicide (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) at first symptoms
Repeat every 7-10 days in wet weather
Remove and dispose of severely affected lower leaves
prevention: Stake or cage plants for airflow

progression.timeline: Days 1-7: small spots on lower leaves

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is a destructive water-mold disease that can wipe out a tomato planting in days under cool wet conditions.
symptoms: Pale water-soaked lesions on leaf margins
Rapid expansion to brown-black necrosis
White fuzzy growth on leaf undersides in humid weather
Greasy-looking brown lesions on fruit
treatment: Apply protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) preventively
Switch to systemic (metalaxyl) once disease appears
Destroy infected plants immediately
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Mountain Magic, Defiant)

progression.timeline: Days 1-3: pale lesions on leaf tips

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Leaf mold (Passalora fulva, formerly Cladosporium) is a fungal disease primarily of greenhouse tomatoes, also field tomatoes in humid weather.
symptoms: Pale yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grayish-purple velvety mold on leaf underside
Leaves yellow, wither, and drop
Reduced yield
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or copper-based fungicide
Improve greenhouse ventilation
Reduce humidity below 85%
prevention: Use resistant cultivars (e.g., Cf-9 gene varieties)

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot (Septoria lycopersici) produces many small circular leaf spots and is one of the most damaging tomato foliar diseases in humid regions.
symptoms: Many small (1-3 mm) circular spots with dark borders and gray centers
Tiny black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) within spots
Lower leaves affected first
Heavy defoliation
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or copper fungicide at first signs
Repeat every 7-10 days during wet weather
Remove infected lower leaves
prevention: Stake plants and maintain wide spacing

progression.timeline: Days 1-7: small circular spots on lower leaves

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: The two-spotted spider mite (Tetranychus urticae) is a tiny arachnid pest that feeds on leaf undersides, causing stippling and webbing.
symptoms: Tiny yellow or white stippling on upper leaf surface
Fine webbing on leaf undersides and between leaves
Bronzing and drying of leaves
Visible tiny mites on the underside of leaves
treatment: Spray plants forcefully with water to dislodge mites
Apply insecticidal soap or horticultural oil
Use predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) in greenhouses
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predators
prevention: Maintain adequate humidity (mites prefer dry, dusty conditions)
Encourage natural predators by minimizing pesticide use

environmentalFactors.sunlight: Full sun is fine; mite damage worse on stressed plants

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot (Corynespora cassiicola) is a fungal disease producing target-like leaf and fruit lesions in warm humid climates.
symptoms: Brown circular leaf spots with concentric rings
Lesions on stems and fruit
Lesions may merge causing leaf blight
Sunken fruit lesions reducing market value
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide
Repeat every 7-10 days in wet weather
Remove severely affected leaves
prevention: Crop rotation away from tomato/cucurbit/papaya for 2 years

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) is a begomovirus transmitted by silverleaf whitefly. It causes severe stunting and yield loss; there is no cure.
symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing of leaf margins
Severe stunting
Reduced leaf size
Flower drop, sterility, and yield collapse
treatment: No cure available
Aggressively manage whitefly vectors with insecticides or yellow sticky traps
Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent spread
prevention: Use UV-reflective mulches to repel whiteflies

progression.timeline: Week 5+: flower drop and yield loss

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV) is a tobamovirus producing mottled foliage and stunting. Highly stable and easily spread by handling.
symptoms: Mottled light and dark green leaves
Distorted, fern-like leaf shape
Stunted growth
Internal browning and reduced fruit quality
treatment: No cure available
Remove and destroy infected plants
Disinfect tools, hands, and stakes after handling suspect plants
prevention: Wash hands with soap before handling plants
Avoid using tobacco products near tomatoes (TMV reservoir)

treatment: No treatment required
